# Urgent Unknowns

Most entrepreneurs don’t fail because they don't have answers. They fail because they didn't asked the right questions soon enough.  

Every new venture sits on a mountain of unknowns: Who are the customers? What do they care about? Will they pay? Can we even reach them? The list is endless. Trying to solve them all at once is paralyzing. Trying to ignore them is fatal. The real trick is to sort the noise into signal — to spot the few unknowns that matter right now.  

Call these your **urgent unknowns**.  


## What’s an Urgent Unknown?  

An urgent unknown is a gap in knowledge that’s (1) critical to survival or progress, and (2) answerable quickly enough to shape your next move.  

- *Critical:* If you’re wrong about it, you could waste months or burn cash.  
- *Answerable:* You can get evidence on it without waiting for years or spending millions.  

Think of it as triage for your uncertainty.  


## Why It Matters  

When you zero in on urgent unknowns, you avoid two common traps:  

- **Boiling the ocean.** Drowning yourself in research on every possible variable, most of which won’t matter yet.  
- **Flying blind.** Charging ahead on assumptions, only to discover later that you were solving the wrong problem.  

Urgent unknowns give you focus. They tell you where to spend your scarce time, money, and attention.  


## How to Spot Yours  

Here’s a quick way to surface your urgent unknowns:  

1. **List the big uncertainties.** Everything you don’t know about customers, product, market, operations. No filter yet.  
2. **Mark what’s most consequential.** Circle the ones that would be fatal or game-changing if you’re wrong.  
3. **Mark what’s most learnable.** Put a star by the ones you could actually get evidence on in the next week or two.  
4. **Find the overlap.** The circled-and-starred unknowns are urgent. Start there.  


::: {.callout-note icon=false title="Shortcut to Urgent Unknowns"}
If you can’t decide what’s urgent, ask yourself:  
*What am I about to spend money or effort on?*  
Whatever assumption that action rests on is your urgent unknown.  
:::


## Sequencing Your Unknowns

Not all unknowns deserve attention at the same time. Urgency is about order.  
For example, when entering a new market with a new product:

1. Validation comes first. Do people outside your immediate circle want something like this at all?
2. Fit comes next. Which version of the product or service best matches what they want?
3. Economics follow. Once demand is clear, does the price cover costs, and can margins scale?
4. Scaling questions come last. Which channels, which packaging, which growth strategies?

Think of your unknowns as a queue. Each one matters, but some can’t be answered until others are settled. Sequencing is how you avoid wasting time answering the wrong question too early.

## Known Unknowns vs. Genuine Unknowns

Sometimes what feels like an “unknown” is really just something you personally don’t know yet — but somebody else already does. That’s not usually an urgent unknown.

Urgent unknowns are the questions no one else can answer for you — the ones that only direct evidence in your specific context will settle.

If the answer already exists in a database, report, or industry benchmark, don’t burn scarce cycles rediscovering it. Accept that you can borrow the knowledge and move on. Save your immediate experiments for the genuine unknowns where no one has the answer yet.

Yes, at some point you’ll need to learn the known answer for yourself. But until then, its urgency is low. In contrast, the **genuine unknowns** — the questions no report or dataset can settle — deserve elevation to your most urgent unknowns.  

::: {.callout-note appearance=minimal title="Mini-Case: ClearSip Bottles"}

ClearSip is a student startup designing reusable water bottles made from biodegradable materials.

Early on, the team listed several uncertainties:

- What percentage of U.S. consumers already own reusable bottles?
- What is the cost of producing biodegradable bottles at scale?
- Will eco-conscious buyers actually switch from their existing bottles to ClearSip?

At first, they treated all three as urgent unknowns. But two of them weren’t:

- The percentage of reusable bottle ownership was already published in consumer research.
- The production costs at scale were documented in supplier catalogs and trade reports.

ClearSip could find both answers in an afternoon of desk research. They didn’t need to run surveys or experiments.

The genuine urgent unknown was the third: Would buyers actually switch? That’s something no database could tell them. They had to test it directly with customers.

*Lesson: Don’t burn cycles reinventing answers that are already known. Save your energy for the questions only you can answer.*
:::


## The Payoff  

Every entrepreneur has more questions than they can possibly answer.  
The discipline is not in collecting questions — it’s in choosing which ones to chase *now*.  

Mastering urgent unknowns keeps you learning on purpose, instead of by accident.  

When you leave this chapter, you should be able to point to your top one or two urgent unknowns. They’ll be the starting point for the next step: turning them into hypotheses you can test.  


<!-- 
1. EduTrack App (EdTech case)

::: {.callout-note title=“Mini-Case: EduTrack”}
EduTrack is a student startup building an app that helps college students track study habits and stay accountable to academic goals.

The team listed several uncertainties:
	•	How many college students already use study-tracking apps?
	•	What is the cost of building an app with basic habit-tracking features?
	•	Will students actually stick with EduTrack instead of dropping it after a week?

Two of these are already known:
	•	Usage rates are available in industry reports.
	•	Development costs are easy to estimate from freelance platforms and app dev firms.

The genuine urgent unknown is persistence: Will students actually keep using the app once the novelty wears off? No database can answer that — only real-world testing will.
:::

⸻

2. FreshCrate Produce (Food packaging / sustainability case)

::: {.callout-note title=“Mini-Case: FreshCrate”}
FreshCrate is designing eco-friendly produce packaging for farmer’s markets.

Their list of uncertainties included:
	•	How many households buy fresh produce weekly?
	•	What’s the cost difference between compostable and plastic packaging at scale?
	•	Will shoppers at farmer’s markets choose the eco-friendly packaging, even if it costs a little more?

The first two are already known — USDA and packaging suppliers publish that data.

The genuine urgent unknown is the third: Will shoppers actually choose eco-friendly packaging when buying produce? That can only be answered through testing in context.
:::

⸻

3. FitNest+ (Student fitness case)

::: {.callout-note title=“Mini-Case: FitNest+”}
FitNest+ is a student venture offering guided group workouts in outdoor campus spaces.

Their uncertainties included:
	•	How many students exercise weekly?
	•	What is the cost of renting simple equipment?
	•	Will students show up consistently to group workouts run by peers?

The first two are already known — surveys and supplier catalogs cover them.

The genuine urgent unknown is the third: Will students actually commit to showing up? That requires live trials, not secondhand data.
:::
-->
